planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm soil; arguably the most widely grown slicing cucumber in American home gardens, selected at Cornell University for disease resistance and consistent productivity.

directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep in warm soil after all frost risk has passed, in hills of 3–4 seeds thinned to 2, or in rows 12 inches apart against a trellis; cucumbers germinate quickly in warm soil and grow fast — do not plant in cold ground. Marketmore 76 was developed at Cornell University and released in 1976 as an improvement on the original Marketmore line, and for decades it has been the benchmark slicing cucumber for home gardeners: a vigorous, disease-resistant vine producing straight, dark green, 8–9 inch fruits with thin, non-bitter skin and crisp, mild white flesh that stays productive over a long harvest window. The variety carries resistance to cucumber mosaic virus, angular leaf spot, powdery mildew, and scab. Pick fruits when uniformly dark green and firm, before any yellowing begins, to encourage continued production; cucumbers left on the vine too long signal the plant to stop setting new fruit.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; one of the oldest American pickling cucumbers, documented since the 1870s, producing short, blocky, thin-skinned fruits ideal for dill pickles and fermented pickles.

directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep after last frost in warm, fertile, well-drained soil; Boston Pickling is a fast-maturing (55–65 days) pickling type that produces very early and keeps producing well if harvested at 2–3 inch length for small dill pickles or 3–4 inch length for whole dill pickles. Boston Pickling is an American heirloom documented since the 1870s, producing short, blocky, light-to-medium green fruits with slightly ridged skin, crisp white flesh, and a small seed cavity that gives pickles an excellent bite; the skin absorbs brine beautifully, producing crunchy pickles that store for months. Harvest at least every two days when production peaks to keep the vine producing; fruits left to yellow dramatically slow new production. Both vine-type and bush selections exist.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm soil; an All-America Selections winner with striking bicolor fruits — yellow with a green tip — and exceptional flavor; bush habit suits small gardens and container growing.

directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep in warm, well-drained soil after last frost; Zephyr is a bush-type summer squash that requires no staking and produces heavily from a compact plant. Zephyr is an All-America Selections award-winning hybrid summer squash with a visually striking bicolor pattern — straight, yellow upper portion with a distinctive lime-green tip — and very nutty, firm flesh with a noticeably drier texture than standard yellow squash that holds up well to sautéing, grilling, and roasting without becoming waterlogged. Harvest at 4–6 inches for peak tenderness and flavor; the compact bush plants are excellent for small gardens where the large rambling vines of traditional squash would be impractical. Powdery mildew tolerance and prolific production make it a reliable summer standby.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; the quintessential Southern summer squash, ubiquitous at roadside stands and farmers' markets across the Gulf Coast; highly heat-tolerant and productive even in zone 9–11 summer conditions.

directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep after last frost in warm, rich, well-drained soil; Yellow Crookneck is one of the fastest-producing and most heat-tolerant summer squashes, making it particularly well-suited to warm and humid Gulf Coast gardens where it produces heavily in spring and again in fall. Yellow Crookneck squash has been grown in American gardens since at least the 1700s — it was listed in American seed catalogs before the Civil War — producing curved, club-shaped yellow fruits with bumpy skin and mild, buttery, slightly nutty flesh that is the quintessential taste of Southern summer gardens; harvest at 4–6 inches when the skin is still tender and seeds are small. In hot climates, a fall planting (direct sow in late summer) often outperforms spring; the plants are relatively short-lived under heat stress but can be succession-planted.

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; a Taiwanese heirloom and one of the most productive and heat-tolerant eggplants available — exceptionally well-suited to Gulf Coast gardens and zone 9–11 conditions where it often produces far better than Italian or Japanese types.

directions: Start seeds at 80°F 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant to warm, rich, well-drained soil after all frost risk has passed. Ping Tung Long is a Taiwanese heirloom eggplant producing clusters of very long (12–14 inch), slender, brilliant purple-lavender fruits with very thin, nearly seedless, tender flesh and exceptionally mild, sweet, non-bitter flavor outstanding for grilling, stir-frying, and braising; the tender skin does not need peeling. This is among the most productive and heat-tolerant eggplants available, thriving through the entire Gulf Coast summer with minimal care, where it sets fruit persistently even through periods of high humidity and heat that cause other varieties to drop flowers. Pick when fruits are shiny and uniformly colored before any dulling occurs, which signals over-maturity.
